---
title: "1.2 Common Pitfalls of Backtesting"
subtitle: "Video lesson slides"
author: "Algorithmic Trading"

format:
  revealjs:
    theme: default
    css: ../../../../styles/reveal.css
    slide-number: true
    controls: true
    progress: true
    transition: slide
    transition-speed: fast
    center: false
    auto-stretch: true
    auto-animate: true
    auto-slide: 9000
    auto-slide-stoppable: true
    show-notes: false
    scrollable: true
    embed-resources: false
execute:
  echo: true
---

## Gross Is Not Tradable

::: columns
::: {.column width="52%"}
Gross returns ask:

**Did the signal point the right way?**
:::

::: {.column width="48%"}
Net returns ask:

**Did the edge survive trading?**
:::
:::

The second question is the only one a portfolio can spend.

::: {.notes}
Start with the contrast. Gross performance is useful for research, but it is
not a trading decision. Net performance is where costs, timing, and capacity
meet the signal.
:::

## The One-Period Test

$$
r^{\text{net}}_t = w_{t-1}r_t - c_t
$$

::: columns
::: {.column width="33%"}
**$w_{t-1}$**

position already known
:::

::: {.column width="33%"}
**$r_t$**

next-period return
:::

::: {.column width="33%"}
**$c_t$**

cost of changing exposure
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
This formula forces two disciplines: position first, return second; trade
first, cost second. Many inflated backtests violate one of those two
relationships.
:::

## Costs Follow Change

$$
\text{Turnover}_t = |w_t - w_{t-1}|
$$

::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}
No change:

$$w_t = w_{t-1}$$

low trading cost
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
Big change:

$$|w_t - w_{t-1}| \uparrow$$

costs rise
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
Costs are not charged because a position exists. They are mostly charged
because the portfolio changes. This is why turnover is the first diagnostic
after gross returns.
:::

## A Useful First Cost Model

$$
c_t = \alpha \cdot \text{Turnover}_t + \beta \cdot \sigma_t
$$

::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Direct costs**

commissions, fees, spread
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
**Liquidity costs**

slippage, impact, volatility
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
This is intentionally simple. Alpha captures costs that scale with trading.
Beta times volatility stands in for market impact or slippage that gets worse
when the market is harder to trade.
:::

## The Curve Is Evidence, Not Verdict

![](../../../../assets/example-pnl.svg){fig-alt="Example cumulative PnL line chart" width="86%"}

Ask what would happen after:

- realistic spread and slippage
- borrow or financing cost
- larger order size

::: {.notes}
The audience will naturally focus on the line. Redirect them to the missing
assumptions. A smooth curve before costs is a hypothesis, not a conclusion.
:::

## Sharpe Can Hide the Pain

::: columns
::: {.column width="52%"}
![](../../../../assets/example-histogram.svg){fig-alt="Example returns histogram" width="100%"}
:::

::: {.column width="48%"}
Check what the average hides:

- tail losses
- skew
- volatility clustering
- rare event dependence
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
The distribution slide prevents the audience from over-reading a smooth
cumulative PnL chart. The same mean return can come with very different tail
risk.
:::

## Capacity Is a Cost Test

::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Small size**

signal may clear the spread
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
**Larger size**

fills move the market
:::
:::

Net returns must be retested at the size you intend to trade.

::: {.notes}
Capacity is where the cost model becomes practical. Doubling capital does not
necessarily double net returns. It can increase impact and make fills less
representative of the backtest.
:::

## Summary Before You Trust Net Returns

::: columns
::: {.column width="33%"}
**Timing**

positions are shifted
:::

::: {.column width="33%"}
**Trading**

costs follow turnover
:::

::: {.column width="33%"}
**Scale**

capacity is stress-tested
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
This summary restates the three ideas the learner should keep: timing,
turnover, and scale. Then move into the closing checklist as the action step.
:::

## Review Before Trust

- Is the position shifted before returns are applied?
- Are costs applied to turnover?
- Are borrow, spread, and slippage visible assumptions?
- Does performance survive realistic capacity constraints?

::: {.notes}
Close by turning the section into a checklist. This makes the next coding or
research step clear.
:::
